subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: YCJA — Who is a Young Person
fact_pattern: Marcus began committing a series of thefts from a convenience store at the age of 11 years and 10 months. He continued stealing on multiple occasions over the following three months. By the time the last theft occurred, Marcus had turned 12. He is now charged with all the thefts in a single information. The Crown takes the position that the YCJA applies to all charges; defence counsel argues it does not apply to the offences committed before Marcus turned 12.
call_of_question: Under s. 2(1) of the Youth Criminal Justice Act, how should the court assess the YCJA's application to Marcus's charges?

options.A: The YCJA applies to all charges because Marcus was under 18 throughout, and the age threshold is assessed at the time of the information, not each individual offence.
options.B: The YCJA applies only to offences committed on or after Marcus's 12th birthday; offences committed while he was under 12 cannot be prosecuted under any criminal law.
options.C: The YCJA applies to all charges because, once a person meets the definition of 'young person' for any offence, the Act applies to all offences in the same proceeding.
options.D: The YCJA does not apply to Marcus at all because he began the offending before he turned 12 and the charges arise from a continuous course of conduct.

explanation: Under s.2(1) of the Youth Criminal Justice Act defines a 'young person' as a person who is twelve years of age or older but under eighteen years of age at the time of the alleged offence. The critical reference point is the age at the time each alleged offence was committed. A child under 12 is below the minimum age of criminal responsibility in Canada and cannot be charged with a criminal offence — neither under the YCJA nor under adult proceedings. Therefore, thefts committed while Marcus was 11 years and 10 months old cannot be prosecuted at all, as he lacked criminal capacity at that time. The YCJA applies only to the offences committed on or after his 12th birthday. Each charge must be assessed individually against the offender's age at the time of that particular offence.
why_A_wrong: Wrong Timeline Trap. The YCJA's age threshold is assessed at the time of each individual offence, not at the time the information is laid. The fact that Marcus was under 18 throughout the entire period does not bring offences committed before his 12th birthday within the YCJA or any other criminal law.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The s. 2(1) definition requires that the person be 12 or older at the time of the alleged offence. Charges arising from conduct before Marcus turned 12 cannot be prosecuted criminally; only offences from his 12th birthday onward fall under the YCJA.
why_C_wrong: General Rule Trap. There is no 'contamination' or 'once qualifying, always qualifying' rule under the YCJA. Each charge is assessed by reference to the accused's age at the time of that specific offence. Meeting the definition for one charge does not bring earlier charges (committed while under 12) within the YCJA.
why_D_wrong: Overstatement Trap. The fact that Marcus was under 12 when some offences occurred does not deprive him of YCJA protection for the offences committed after he turned 12. The Act applies on a charge-by-charge basis. Offences committed after his 12th birthday are within the YCJA regardless of the earlier offending.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Definition of Young Person — YCJA s. 2(1)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 2(1)

tested_concepts: YCJA s. 2(1) definition of young person
age at time of offence — not at time of information
minimum age of criminal responsibility — under 12 cannot be charged
charge-by-charge age assessment

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 14-year-old is caught shoplifting for the first time. The value of the stolen goods was $30. The investigating police officer is considering how to handle the matter without a formal charge.
call_of_question: Under the YCJA, which of the following best describes the framework for extra-judicial measures that a police officer may use in this situation?

options.A: A police officer has no authority to deal with a young person without laying a formal charge; all youth matters must proceed to court.
options.B: Extra-judicial measures are available only if the young person has no prior criminal record.
options.C: Extra-judicial measures — such as warnings, cautions, and referrals to programs — are available and are preferred over charges for first-time and minor offenders; a charge should be a last resort.
options.D: Extra-judicial measures require the consent of the victim before they can be used.

explanation: Under ss.4-12 of the YCJA establish a robust framework of extra-judicial measures (EJMs) as the preferred response for young persons, particularly first-time and minor offenders. Section 4 states that extra-judicial measures are often the most appropriate and effective way to address young persons' offending behaviour without formal court proceedings. EJMs include police warnings, police cautions, Crown cautions, referrals to community programs, and extrajudicial sanctions (EJS) for more serious cases. Section 6 emphasizes that police should first consider whether an EJM is sufficient. The YCJA philosophy is that a charge should be a last resort, reserved for cases where EJMs are inadequate.
why_A_wrong: Police have broad authority to use EJMs without laying a formal charge. The YCJA expressly directs police to consider EJMs first (s. 6). A formal charge is a last resort, not the default.
why_B_wrong: EJMs are available even if the young person has a prior record, though a prior record may affect the appropriate type of EJM. The YCJA does not restrict EJMs to first-time offenders, though first-time status makes EJMs particularly appropriate.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Sections 4-12 of the YCJA establish EJMs as the preferred response for young persons, particularly first-time and minor offenders, with formal charges as a last resort.
why_D_wrong: Victim consent is not a general prerequisite for EJMs. Some EJMs (particularly extrajudicial sanctions involving victim-offender conferencing) may involve victim participation, but victim consent is not required for police warnings, cautions, or referrals.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Extra-Judicial Measures — YCJA s. 4-12
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 4-12

tested_concepts: YCJA EJM framework
police discretion to use EJMs
charge as last resort principle
types of EJMs
YCJA rehabilitative philosophy

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Statement of a Young Person — YCJA s. 146
fact_pattern: A 15-year-old suspect is taken to the police station for questioning regarding an armed robbery. Before questioning begins, police explain the caution and the right to retain and instruct counsel. The youth says he understands but declines a lawyer. He then makes an inculpatory statement.
call_of_question: Under s. 146 of the YCJA, what additional step was required before police could take this young person's statement?

options.A: Police must have a parent or adult relative present during questioning, and if no parent is available, a responsible adult must be contacted.
options.B: No additional steps were required beyond the standard police caution and s. 10(b) Charter right to counsel.
options.C: Police must have obtained written consent from a parent or guardian before questioning a young person.
options.D: Police must advise the young person of the right to have a parent or other adult person present during questioning, and give the young person a reasonable opportunity to consult with that person.

explanation: Under s.146(2) of the YCJA establishes specific requirements for a young person's statement to be admissible. Before the statement is made, the person who took the statement must have clearly explained to the young person, in language appropriate to their age and understanding: (a) the nature of the offence and the circumstances giving rise to it, (b) the right to retain and instruct counsel, (c) the right to have a parent or other appropriate person present, and (d) that the statement may be used as evidence. Section 146(2)(b) requires that the young person be given a reasonable opportunity to consult with counsel AND with a parent or other appropriate adult before making the statement. The failure to advise of and provide this opportunity is a basis to exclude the statement.
why_A_wrong: Section 146 does not require that a parent or adult actually be present during questioning. It requires that the young person be advised of the right to have such a person present and given a reasonable opportunity to consult. If the young person waives this right, the statement may still be admissible.
why_B_wrong: Additional steps beyond the standard caution and Charter right to counsel are required under s. 146 YCJA. The Act provides enhanced protections for young persons, including advisement of the right to have a parent or other adult present and the opportunity to consult with them.
why_C_wrong: Written consent from a parent or guardian is not required as a condition for taking a young person's statement under s.146. The requirement is advisement and reasonable opportunity to consult — the young person may waive parental presence. Written parental consent is not the statutory requirement.
why_D_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 146(2) requires police to advise the young person of the right to have a parent or other adult person present and to give the young person a reasonable opportunity to consult with that person before the statement is made.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Statement of Young Person — YCJA s. 146
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 146(2)

tested_concepts: s. 146(2) YCJA requirements for youth statements
right to have parent/adult present
reasonable opportunity to consult
youth-specific protections beyond standard Charter caution

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 16-year-old is convicted of robbery under the YCJA. The sentencing judge is deciding among available youth sentences. Crown counsel submits that the appropriate sanction is a custodial youth sentence.
call_of_question: Under s. 39(1) of the YCJA, which of the following is the threshold requirement before a youth court may impose a custodial sentence?

options.A: The young person must have a prior record of at least one previous youth sentence.
options.B: The offence must be a designated violent offence as listed in the schedule to the YCJA.
options.C: The court must be satisfied that the offence is serious enough, the young person has a history that indicates a pattern of offending, or the exceptional circumstances of the case warrant custody; non-custodial sentences must have been considered inadequate.
options.D: The court requires the written consent of the youth's parents before imposing custody.

explanation: Under s.39(1) of the YCJA restricts custodial sentences for young persons. A youth court shall not commit a young person to custody unless one of the following conditions is met: (a) the young person has committed a violent offence; (b) the young person has failed to comply with non-custodial sentences; (c) the young person has committed an indictable offence for which an adult would be liable to imprisonment for more than two years and has a history indicating a pattern of extrajudicial sanctions or non-custodial sentences; or (d) in exceptional cases where the young person has committed an indictable offence and the aggravating circumstances of the offence justify custody. Non-custodial sentences must have been considered and found inadequate before custody may be ordered (s.39(2)).
why_A_wrong: A prior record is not required before custodial sentences can be imposed. A young person with no prior record who commits a violent offence may receive custody under s.39(1)(a). Conversely, a prior record alone does not automatically lead to custody.
why_B_wrong: There is no 'scheduled' list of designated violent offences as a threshold for YCJA custody. The violence requirement is whether the offence is a 'violent offence' as defined in s.2 YCJA (causing, attempting to cause, or threatening to cause bodily harm), not a formal schedule.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 39(1) provides the threshold conditions for custodial youth sentences, and s.39(2) requires the court to consider non-custodial sentences as inadequate before imposing custody.
why_D_wrong: Parental consent is not required before a youth court may impose a custodial sentence. The YCJA governs the young person's sentence; parents participate in proceedings (with notification rights) but do not have a veto over sentencing outcomes.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Youth Custodial Sentence Threshold — YCJA s. 39
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 39(1), s. 39(2), s. 42

tested_concepts: s. 39(1) threshold for youth custody
non-custodial sentence must be considered inadequate
violent offence as custody gateway
YCJA custody restrictions

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 17-year-old is charged with first degree murder. The Crown applies for an adult sentence under the YCJA, arguing the youth sentence would not be sufficient to hold the accused accountable.
call_of_question: Under the YCJA, which party bears the burden of proof on an adult sentence application, and what is the test the court applies?

options.A: The Crown bears the burden of proving that a youth sentence would not be adequate to hold the young person accountable, on a balance of probabilities.
options.B: The young person bears the burden of proving that a youth sentence is adequate, on a balance of probabilities.
options.C: The burden shifts to the young person once the Crown establishes a prima facie case for adult sentencing.
options.D: There is no burden of proof — the court makes the determination based on the pre-sentence report.

explanation: Under s. 72(1) of the YCJA (as amended), the youth justice court shall order that an adult sentence be imposed if it is satisfied that: (a) the presumption of diminished moral blameworthiness or culpability is rebutted; and (b) a youth sentence imposed under s.42 would not be of sufficient length to hold the young person accountable for their offending behaviour. The burden of proof on an adult sentence application falls on the Crown, who must prove, on a balance of probabilities, that a youth sentence would be inadequate. This reflects the YCJA's presumption that youth sentences are generally appropriate and that adult sentences are the exception.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 72(1) YCJA, the Crown bears the burden of proving, on a balance of probabilities, that a youth sentence would be inadequate to hold the young person accountable.
why_B_wrong: The burden is not on the young person to prove that a youth sentence is adequate. The YCJA presumes youth sentences are appropriate; the Crown must rebut this by proving adult sentencing is warranted.
why_C_wrong: There is no shifting burden mechanism in the YCJA adult sentencing framework. The Crown bears the burden throughout the application. There is no prima facie standard that triggers a shift.
why_D_wrong: The court does not operate without a burden of proof at an adult sentencing application. The determination is made on evidence and submissions, with the Crown bearing the balance of probabilities burden. The PSR informs the analysis but does not replace the burden of proof framework.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Adult Sentence Application — YCJA s. 64, s. 72
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 64, s. 72(1)

tested_concepts: adult sentence burden on Crown
balance of probabilities standard
s. 72(1) test — youth sentence inadequacy
YCJA presumption of youth sentence appropriateness

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Youth Records — Access and Sealing — YCJA s. 119-128
fact_pattern: Tariq was convicted at age 16 of theft under $5,000 and received a reprimand as his youth sentence. He is now 22 years old and has had no further contact with the justice system. A prospective employer conducts a criminal record check. Tariq's youth conviction was recorded in the RCMP repository.
call_of_question: Under the YCJA, what is the status of Tariq's youth record at age 22, given that he received a reprimand and has been offence-free?

options.A: The youth record remains accessible indefinitely because it was entered in the RCMP national repository at the time of conviction.
options.B: The youth record is sealed and inaccessible; the access period for a reprimand ends three months after the reprimand was pronounced, and Tariq has been offence-free for years beyond that.
options.C: The youth record's access period expired three years after the reprimand was pronounced because youth records for all youth sentences are accessible for a minimum of three years under the YCJA.
options.D: The youth record can only be sealed if Tariq makes a formal court application after turning 18 to have the record expunged.

explanation: Under s. 119(2)(a) of the YCJA, where the young person received a reprimand as the youth sentence, the period during which persons are permitted access to records expires three months after the reprimand was pronounced. After this access period closes, the record is sealed and persons listed in s.119(1) can no longer access it. Since Tariq received a reprimand at age 16 and is now 22, the three-month access period has long expired. The record is effectively sealed. For more serious youth sentences, the access period is longer (e.g., three years after a non-custodial sentence is completed, five years after custody sentences end). The scheme reflects the YCJA principle that youth records should not follow young persons into adulthood.
why_A_wrong: Youth records in the RCMP repository are not permanently accessible. The YCJA establishes time-limited access periods that depend on the sentence imposed. A reprimand carries only a three-month access period under s.119(2)(a), after which the record is sealed.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s.119(2)(a) YCJA, the access period for a youth record where the sentence was a reprimand expires three months after the reprimand was pronounced. At age 22, Tariq's record is sealed.
why_C_wrong: C confuses the access period for a reprimand with the access period for other, more serious youth sentences. The Sentence-Type-Confusion Trap: the YCJA establishes different access periods depending on the type of youth sentence imposed. Under s. 119(2)(a), the access period for a reprimand is only three months from the date it was pronounced — not three years. A three-year access period applies to conditional discharges (s. 119(2)(b)). Tariq received a reprimand, not a conditional discharge, so the three-month period applies and his record has been sealed for years.
why_D_wrong: No formal court application is required to seal a youth record. Sealing is automatic once the access period expires under s.119(2). The young person need not apply to court — the record simply becomes inaccessible once the statutory period ends.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Youth Records — Access Periods — YCJA s. 119
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 119(2)(a), s. 128

tested_concepts: s. 119(2)(a) — three-month access period for reprimand
youth records seal automatically
access period varies with sentence type
YCJA youth records protection principle

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Deferred Custody and Supervision Order — YCJA s. 42(2)(p)
fact_pattern: A 17-year-old is convicted under the YCJA of assault causing bodily harm, which is a violent offence. The sentencing judge is considering a deferred custody and supervision order (DCSO) as an alternative to immediate custody. Defence counsel argues that a DCSO would allow the accused to remain in the community while completing school.
call_of_question: Under the YCJA, which of the following is a statutory prerequisite before the court may impose a deferred custody and supervision order?

options.A: A DCSO may only be imposed for non-violent offences; the violent nature of assault causing bodily harm categorically bars a DCSO.
options.B: A DCSO is available for any offence and requires only that the accused consent to the conditions.
options.C: A DCSO may be imposed only if the sentence that would otherwise be imposed is custody, and the court must be satisfied the young person's presence in the community would not create an undue risk to public safety.
options.D: A DCSO requires the Crown's consent and a supporting recommendation in the pre-sentence report.

explanation: Under Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 42(2)(p), s. 42(5), A deferred custody and supervision order (DCSO) under s. 42(2)(p) of the YCJA allows a court to impose a period of community supervision in lieu of immediate custody. The conditions for a DCSO are set out in s. 42(5): (a) the young person is not found guilty of a presumptive offence or an offence for which an adult would be liable to more than two years' imprisonment would be inadequate; and (b) the court must be satisfied that the DCSO is consistent with the purpose and principles of youth sentencing and the young person's presence in the community on the terms and conditions proposed would not create an undue risk to public safety. The DCSO is functionally a suspended custody sentence — if breached, the young person can be brought back before the court and committed to custody. Assault causing bodily harm is not excluded categorically, though it is a violent offence that will require careful consideration of the undue risk criterion.
why_A_wrong: The YCJA does not categorically bar DCSOs for violent offences. Assault causing bodily harm is a violent offence, but the DCSO can still be considered if the undue risk to public safety criterion is satisfied. The bar relates to 'presumptive offences' (serious violent offences like murder and manslaughter) and offences carrying maximum adult sentences that make youth sentences inadequate.
why_B_wrong: A DCSO is not available for any offence simply upon accused consent. The court must independently be satisfied that the public safety criterion is met and that the DCSO is consistent with the purpose and principles of youth sentencing. Accused consent to conditions is part of implementation, not the threshold test.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 42(5) YCJA, a DCSO requires that custody would otherwise be the appropriate sentence and that the young person's presence in the community on the DCSO terms would not create an undue risk to public safety.
why_D_wrong: Crown consent is not a statutory prerequisite for imposing a DCSO. The sentencing judge may impose a DCSO without Crown consent, provided the statutory criteria are met. A PSR may be before the court but a recommendation favouring DCSO is not a precondition.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Deferred Custody and Supervision Order — YCJA s. 42(2)(p), s. 42(5)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 42(2)(p), s. 42(5)

tested_concepts: DCSO prerequisites under s. 42(5) YCJA
undue risk to public safety test
DCSO as deferred custody sentence
violent offence and DCSO eligibility

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Statements by Young Persons — YCJA s. 146 and the Operating Mind Requirement
fact_pattern: A 14-year-old, Jayla, is arrested at 2:00 a.m. on suspicion of robbery. She has a diagnosed intellectual disability that places her cognitive functioning at approximately a 10-year-old level. Police read Jayla her rights and the YCJA waiver rights in plain language, and she nodded. They then advised her she could speak to a lawyer or parent; she said 'I don't need them.' Police questioned Jayla for 90 minutes, during which she made incriminating admissions. At the voir dire, a psychologist testifies that Jayla likely did not understand the consequences of speaking to police without counsel. Police testify they believed she understood and waived voluntarily.
call_of_question: Under s. 146 of the YCJA, which element of admissibility is most directly in contest on these facts, and what is the governing standard?

options.A: The voluntariness of the statement — assessed under the common law confessions rule — is the only relevant test; the young person's intellectual disability goes only to weight.
options.B: The operating mind requirement — whether Jayla had the cognitive capacity to understand what she was saying and its consequences — is the contested element; it is assessed subjectively based on Jayla's actual state of mind, not on what the police reasonably believed.
options.C: The parental/counsel notification requirement is the sole dispositive issue; because Jayla waived notification, the statement is admissible and no further inquiry is necessary.
options.D: The statement is automatically excluded because the police failed to seek a judicial order authorizing questioning of a young person with an intellectual disability before proceeding.

explanation: Under s.146 of the YCJA imposes a layered admissibility framework for statements by young persons that is more protective than the general common law confessions rule. In addition to common law voluntariness, the YCJA requires: (i) the young person was informed of their right to retain counsel, have a parent/adult present, and was advised of the right to silence; (ii) the young person had a reasonable opportunity to consult counsel or an adult; and (iii) the young person waived those rights. The hardest element on these facts is the 'operating mind' requirement — a component of voluntariness that asks whether the young person had the cognitive capacity to understand what they were saying and appreciate the consequences of making a statement. The standard is subjective: the court must assess Jayla's actual state of mind and cognitive capacity, not merely what a reasonable person in her position would understand, and not what the police believed she understood. The psychologist's evidence that Jayla likely did not grasp the consequences directly challenges operating mind. A police belief that she understood is legally insufficient if the subjective operating mind threshold is not met.
why_A_wrong: While the common law confessions rule (voluntariness) applies to young persons, section 146 YCJA adds protections beyond the common law. The intellectual disability is not merely a weight issue — where it undermines the operating mind requirement (a component of voluntariness assessed subjectively), it can exclude the statement entirely. Characterizing the disability as 'going only to weight' understates the legal significance. This is an Understatement Trap.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The operating mind element of voluntariness is directly in issue. The governing standard is subjective — the court assesses Jayla's actual cognitive state and understanding, not a police officer's good-faith belief about it.
why_C_wrong: Jayla's purported waiver of notification does not end the inquiry. Even if the YCJA notification requirements are technically satisfied, the operating mind component of voluntariness remains a separate, independent requirement. A waiver given without operating mind is itself invalid. Treating waiver as the sole dispositive issue is a Premature Escalation Trap — it short-circuits the voluntariness analysis.
why_D_wrong: There is no provision in the YCJA or Criminal Code requiring police to obtain a judicial order before questioning a young person with an intellectual disability. No such pre-authorization mechanism exists; the admissibility of any resulting statement is assessed at a voir dire after the fact. This option invents a procedure — a Misapplied Tool trap.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Statements by Young Persons — YCJA s. 146
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 146; R. v. L.T.H., 2008 SCC 49

tested_concepts: s. 146 YCJA statements framework
operating mind requirement — subjective standard
intellectual disability and operating mind
police belief insufficient — subjective test applies
waiver does not cure absent operating mind
YCJA protections beyond common law confessions rule

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Youth Records — Deemed Election and Publication Prohibition — YCJA s. 110
fact_pattern: A 16-year-old is charged with aggravated assault. A local newspaper reporter attends the youth court proceedings and wishes to publish a story identifying the young person by name, including a photo. The young person has not been sentenced to an adult sentence.
call_of_question: Under s. 110 of the YCJA, which of the following correctly states the default rule regarding publication of identifying information about a young person who is the subject of youth court proceedings?

options.A: Publication of identifying information about a young person is prohibited unless the young person consents and the court orders publication.
options.B: Publication of identifying information about a young person who has not received an adult sentence is prohibited; however, the young person may themselves apply to the court for an order permitting publication.
options.C: Publication of identifying information is prohibited as a default rule, but becomes automatically permitted once the young person turns 18.
options.D: There is no prohibition on publication because youth court proceedings are open to the public.

explanation: Under s.110(1) of the YCJA prohibits any person from publishing the name of a young person, or any other information related to a young person, if it would identify the young person as a person dealt with under the Act. This prohibition applies to young persons who have not received an adult sentence. However, s. 110(2) and (3) create limited exceptions. Notably, s. 110(2)(b) provides that a young person who is 18 years of age or older may apply to the youth justice court for an order permitting publication. Also, publication may be permitted if a young person is at large and dangerous (s. 110(4)), or in certain other exceptional circumstances. The publication ban is a default rule reflecting the YCJA's principle of protecting young persons' privacy to facilitate rehabilitation.
why_A_wrong: The young person's consent alone is not sufficient to permit publication — court authorization under the YCJA's framework is required. The process is not simply consent-based; it involves a court order in specific circumstances set out in the Act.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 110(1) YCJA, publication of identifying information is prohibited by default. The young person (if 18 or older) may apply for a court order permitting publication under s. 110(2)(b), as one of the limited exceptions.
why_C_wrong: Turning 18 does not automatically permit publication. The publication ban continues after the young person turns 18. Exceptions require a court order or other specific circumstances set out in the YCJA, not merely the passage of time until adulthood.
why_D_wrong: Youth court proceedings may be open to the public but publication of identifying information is still prohibited under s. 110. Open court and publication bans can coexist — observers may attend, but publishing identifying information about the young person remains prohibited absent a court order or statutory exception.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Publication Prohibition — YCJA s. 110
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 110(1), s. 110(2)(b)

tested_concepts: s. 110(1) YCJA default publication prohibition
exceptions to youth publication ban
young person may apply for publication order
open court vs publication ban distinction

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 16-year-old with one prior police caution is charged with theft over $5,000, an indictable offence. The Crown is considering diverting the matter through a formal extrajudicial sanctions (EJS) program under the YCJA rather than proceeding with the charge.
call_of_question: Under s. 10 of the YCJA, which of the following is a mandatory precondition before an extrajudicial sanction may be used?

options.A: The young person must have no prior criminal record or prior extrajudicial measure of any kind.
options.B: The young person must accept responsibility for the act or omission that forms the basis of the offence alleged.
options.C: The Crown must consent in writing to the EJS and authorize the specific program to be used.
options.D: The offence must be a summary conviction offence — extrajudicial sanctions are unavailable for indictable offences.

explanation: Under s.10(2) of the YCJA sets out the mandatory conditions that must be satisfied before an extrajudicial sanction (EJS) may be used. Section 10(2)(b) requires that the young person accept responsibility for the act or omission that forms the basis of the offence alleged. Other conditions under s. 10(2) include: (a) the sanction must be part of a program authorized by the Attorney General; (c) the young person must freely consent; (d) the young person must have had the opportunity to consult with counsel before consenting; and (f) the acceptance of responsibility is not admissible in evidence against the young person in any civil or criminal proceeding. Notably, EJS is not categorically barred for indictable offences — there is no offence-type restriction. A prior police caution does not automatically foreclose EJS, though it is a relevant factor.
why_A_wrong: A prior extrajudicial measure does not categorically bar a subsequent EJS. Section 10(2) does not require a clean record. A prior police caution is a relevant factor in assessing adequacy, but not an automatic disqualifier.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 10(2)(b) YCJA, acceptance of responsibility for the act or omission forming the basis of the offence is a mandatory precondition for EJS.
why_C_wrong: Individual Crown written consent for each EJS use is not a statutory precondition. The requirement under s. 10(2)(a) is that the EJS program be authorized by the Attorney General — this is satisfied by program-level authorization, not case-by-case Crown consent.
why_D_wrong: Extrajudicial sanctions are not limited to summary conviction offences. The YCJA contains no categorical offence-type bar on EJS. The nature of the offence informs whether EJS is adequate and appropriate, but indictable offences are not categorically excluded.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Extrajudicial Sanctions — YCJA s. 10
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 10(2)

tested_concepts: s. 10(2)(b) acceptance of responsibility as mandatory EJS precondition
EJS available for indictable offences
distinction between EJS and EJM
EJS conditions under s. 10(2)

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Pre-Trial Detention of Young Persons — YCJA s. 29
fact_pattern: A 14-year-old is arrested and charged with assault. She comes from a chaotic home environment and has no stable housing. A youth worker suggests that pre-trial detention would benefit her because a youth facility would give her access to counselling and programming unavailable in the community. The Crown seeks detention on this basis.
call_of_question: Under s. 29(1) of the YCJA, which of the following correctly states the restriction on pre-trial detention of a young person?

options.A: A young person may be detained before trial if detention would benefit the young person by providing access to programs or services unavailable in the community.
options.B: A young person may not be detained before trial unless a custodial youth sentence could be imposed and the offence appears on a statutory schedule.
options.C: A young person shall not be detained in a youth custody facility as a substitute for appropriate child protection, mental health, or other social measures.
options.D: Pre-trial detention is unavailable for young persons; release on bail must always be granted to a person under 18 years of age.

explanation: Under s.29(1) of the YCJA provides that a youth justice court judge or a justice shall not detain a young person in custody prior to sentencing as a substitute for appropriate child protection, mental health, or other social measures. This provision codifies the principle that the youth justice system must not serve as a surrogate for other social service systems that have failed the young person. Even if detention would provide beneficial programming, using pre-trial detention for that purpose is expressly prohibited by s. 29(1). The youth justice court applies Criminal Code bail principles as modified by YCJA principles of restraint, but the s. 29(1) prohibition is an additional statutory bar that overrides access-to-services rationales.
why_A_wrong: Section 29(1) expressly prohibits using pre-trial detention as a substitute for social measures, even when detention would provide beneficial services. Detaining a young person to access counselling or treatment programs is precisely the impermissible use that s. 29(1) bars.
why_B_wrong: The YCJA does not restrict pre-trial detention to a specific schedule of offences. Pre-trial detention of young persons is governed by the Criminal Code bail provisions as modified by the YCJA, with the s. 29(1) prohibition as an additional restriction. There is no offence-schedule requirement.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 29(1) YCJA prohibits detaining a young person as a substitute for child protection, mental health, or other social measures. Using detention to access counselling services is the paradigm case s. 29(1) bars.
why_D_wrong: Pre-trial detention of young persons is available — it is not categorically prohibited. The YCJA modifies but does not eliminate Criminal Code bail provisions. Young persons can be detained on primary, secondary, or tertiary grounds, subject to the restrictions in s. 29(1) and the principle of restraint.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Pre-Trial Detention of Young Persons — YCJA s. 29
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 29(1)

tested_concepts: s. 29(1) YCJA — no detention as substitute for social measures
detention impermissible for beneficial services rationale
YCJA principle of restraint in pre-trial detention
youth justice system not a surrogate for other systems

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Publication Ban on Young Victim or Witness — YCJA s. 111
fact_pattern: A 15-year-old victim of a sexual assault is the complainant in a proceeding against an adult accused. A journalist plans to publish a story about the case and asks whether publishing the victim's name is permitted.
call_of_question: Under s. 111 of the YCJA, what is the default rule regarding publication of information that would identify a young person as a victim or witness in any proceeding?

options.A: There is no restriction on publishing the identity of a young person who is a victim because only the accused receives a publication ban under the YCJA.
options.B: The publication ban under s. 111 only applies to young persons who are witnesses, not victims.
options.C: No person shall publish the name of a young person, or any other information related to a young person, if it would identify the young person as a victim or witness in a proceeding; but the court may lift the ban on application.
options.D: The publication ban on young victims applies only in youth court proceedings, not in adult criminal proceedings.

explanation: Under s.111(1) of the YCJA provides that no person shall publish the name of a young person, or any other information related to a young person, if it would identify the young person as a victim or witness in any proceeding under the YCJA or any offence under any other Act of Parliament. This publication ban is distinct from the accused publication ban in s. 110 and applies regardless of the court in which the proceedings are held. Under s. 111(2), the youth justice court judge may, after taking into account the interests of the young person and the public interest in disclosure, make an order permitting the publication. The ban on publishing identifying information about young victims and witnesses is broader in scope than the accused ban — it applies to proceedings under any federal legislation, not just YCJA proceedings.
why_A_wrong: Section 111 extends the YCJA publication ban protection to young victims and witnesses, not just accused young persons. The s. 110 ban covers accused young persons; s. 111 separately covers young victims and witnesses. Both bans exist in the YCJA.
why_B_wrong: Section 111 applies to both young victims and young witnesses — it is not limited to witnesses only. The section protects 'a young person ... as a victim or witness.' Both categories receive the same publication protection.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 111(1) prohibits publication of identifying information about young victims and witnesses in any proceeding. The court may lift the ban under s. 111(2) on application, balancing the young person's interests and the public interest.
why_D_wrong: Section 111 is not limited to youth court proceedings. It applies to any proceeding under the YCJA or any offence under any other federal Act. A young victim in an adult criminal proceeding (as in this fact pattern) is protected by s. 111, which extends beyond youth court.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Publication Ban — Young Victim or Witness — YCJA s. 111
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 111(1), s. 111(2)

tested_concepts: s. 111 YCJA publication ban on young victims and witnesses
distinction between s. 110 (accused) and s. 111 (victim/witness)
court may lift ban under s. 111(2)
ban applies beyond youth court proceedings

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 16-year-old is serving a 12-month custodial youth sentence. She has made significant progress in her rehabilitation program. The youth facility staff recommend that she be granted temporary absences to attend vocational training in the community as part of a structured reintegration plan.
call_of_question: Under s. 91 of the YCJA, who has authority to authorize a reintegration leave from a youth custody facility, and for what purposes may such leave be granted?

options.A: Only a youth justice court judge may authorize reintegration leave, and only for medical emergencies.
options.B: The provincial director has authority to authorize reintegration leave for purposes including medical, humanitarian, or rehabilitative reasons, including to attend educational or vocational training.
options.C: Reintegration leave may only be authorized in the last 30 days of the custodial portion of the sentence.
options.D: The young person must apply to the Parole Board of Canada for temporary absence, which is governed by the Corrections and Conditional Release Act, not the YCJA.

explanation: Under s.91 of the YCJA provides that the provincial director may, with the consent of the young person and of a parent of the young person if the young person is under 18 years of age, authorize a reintegration leave from a youth custody facility. Under s. 91(1), the provincial director may authorize leave for specific purposes including: (a) medical or compassionate reasons; (b) rehabilitative purposes, including attending educational, vocational, or work programs; and (c) reintegration into the community. Reintegration leave is a YCJA mechanism entirely separate from the federal Corrections and Conditional Release Act, which governs adult federal offenders. The YCJA's emphasis on rehabilitation supports structured community access during the custodial sentence to facilitate the young person's transition back to the community.
why_A_wrong: Section 91 grants authority to the provincial director, not the youth justice court, to authorize reintegration leave. The purposes extend well beyond medical emergencies to include rehabilitative and community reintegration purposes. Court approval is not required for reintegration leave under s. 91.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 91(1) YCJA, the provincial director may authorize reintegration leave for medical, humanitarian, rehabilitative, and reintegration purposes — including attending vocational or educational programs in the community.
why_C_wrong: The YCJA does not restrict reintegration leave to the final 30 days of the custodial portion of the sentence. Leave can be authorized at any point during the custodial sentence when the provincial director determines it is appropriate for the young person's rehabilitation and reintegration.
why_D_wrong: Youth offenders under the YCJA are not subject to the Corrections and Conditional Release Act for reintegration leave purposes. The YCJA contains its own reintegration leave framework in s. 91, administered by the provincial director. The CCRA applies to adult federal offenders, not young persons under the YCJA.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Reintegration Leave — YCJA s. 91
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 91(1)

tested_concepts: s. 91(1) YCJA — provincial director authorizes reintegration leave
purposes of reintegration leave — medical, rehabilitative, community
YCJA reintegration leave distinct from CCRA
parental consent required if under 18

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A youth justice court judge is determining the appropriate response for a 15-year-old charged with a first offence of mischief. The judge considers the underlying principles of the YCJA to guide the decision.
call_of_question: Under s. 3 of the YCJA, which of the following is a declared principle of the youth criminal justice system?

options.A: Young persons must be held accountable through intervention that is proportionate to the seriousness of the offence and the degree of responsibility of the young person.
options.B: Young persons who commit offences should be treated identically to adult offenders to promote uniformity in the criminal justice system.
options.C: The primary objective of the youth criminal justice system is general deterrence of youth crime in the community.
options.D: Parents bear criminal responsibility for offences committed by young persons under their care.

explanation: Under s.3(1)(b)(ii) of the YCJA establishes that the youth criminal justice system must ensure that young persons are subject to meaningful consequences and are held accountable through interventions that are proportionate to the seriousness of their offences and the degree of their responsibility. The Declaration of Principle in s. 3 includes: rehabilitation and reintegration of young persons; fair and proportionate accountability; protection of the public; special considerations for young persons due to their reduced moral culpability and greater needs; timely intervention; and reinforcement of societal values. The YCJA explicitly rejects identical treatment with adults and emphasizes individualized, rehabilitative responses. General deterrence is not the primary objective — rehabilitation and reintegration are central.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 3(1)(b)(ii) YCJA declares that young persons are to be held accountable through interventions proportionate to the seriousness of the offence and the degree of their responsibility.
why_B_wrong: The YCJA explicitly does not treat young persons identically to adults. Section 3(1)(b) recognizes young persons' reduced moral culpability, developmental differences, and greater needs. The separate YCJA regime exists precisely because youth justice is distinct from adult criminal justice.
why_C_wrong: General deterrence is not the primary declared objective of the YCJA. Section 3(1)(a) states the purpose is to protect the public by holding young persons accountable through rehabilitation, reintegration, and meaningful consequences. General deterrence — while relevant in adult sentencing — is given significantly less weight in youth sentencing.
why_D_wrong: Parents do not bear criminal responsibility for offences of their young persons under the YCJA. The YCJA does impose obligations on parents (e.g., notice, participation in proceedings), but does not make parents criminally responsible. The young person bears individual accountability for their own acts.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Declaration of Principle — YCJA s. 3
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 3(1)

tested_concepts: s. 3(1) YCJA Declaration of Principle
proportionate accountability as YCJA principle
rehabilitation and reintegration as primary goals
general deterrence not primary in youth justice

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Intensive Rehabilitative Custody and Supervision — YCJA s. 42(2)(r)
fact_pattern: A 17-year-old is convicted of manslaughter. The young person has a diagnosed mental disorder that contributed significantly to the offending behaviour. The Crown applies for an intensive rehabilitative custody and supervision (IRCS) order, arguing it is the most appropriate sentence.
call_of_question: Under s. 42(7) of the YCJA, which of the following is a prerequisite before the court may impose an intensive rehabilitative custody and supervision (IRCS) order?

options.A: The offence must be a first-degree murder, second-degree murder, attempted murder, manslaughter, or aggravated sexual assault, and the young person must be suffering from a mental illness or disorder, a psychological disorder, or an emotional disturbance.
options.B: The IRCS order requires the prior consent of the young person and approval of the provincial director for the relevant treatment program.
options.C: The IRCS order is available for any serious violent offence and requires only a pre-sentence report recommending treatment.
options.D: The IRCS order requires that an adult sentence application has been made and rejected before it may be considered as an alternative.

explanation: Under s.42(7) of the YCJA establishes the conditions for an intensive rehabilitative custody and supervision (IRCS) order. The court may impose an IRCS order only if: (a) the young person has been convicted of a first-degree murder, second-degree murder, attempted murder, manslaughter, or aggravated sexual assault; (b) the young person is suffering from a mental illness or disorder, a psychological disorder, or an emotional disturbance; (c) a plan of treatment or set of programmes has been developed for the young person, and there are reasonable grounds to believe the plan or programme would reduce the risk of the young person repeating the offence or committing a serious violent offence; and (d) the provincial director has determined that an intensive rehabilitative custody and supervision program is available to the young person. IRCS is a specialized custodial sentence designed to address mental-health-linked serious violent offending in young persons.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 42(7) YCJA requires conviction of one of the enumerated serious violent offences AND that the young person be suffering from a mental illness, disorder, or psychological/emotional disturbance, along with a viable treatment plan.
why_B_wrong: While the provincial director's determination that a program is available is required (s. 42(7)(d)), the young person's personal consent is not listed as a mandatory precondition in s. 42(7). Consent is relevant to treatment participation but is not part of the s. 42(7) threshold test.
why_C_wrong: IRCS is not available for any serious violent offence. Section 42(7)(a) limits IRCS to the five specific enumerated offences: first-degree murder, second-degree murder, attempted murder, manslaughter, and aggravated sexual assault. A pre-sentence report alone does not satisfy the preconditions.
why_D_wrong: An adult sentence application is not a precondition for IRCS. IRCS is an independent youth sentence option. The court may consider IRCS without a prior adult sentencing application having been made or rejected.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Intensive Rehabilitative Custody and Supervision — YCJA s. 42(7)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 42(2)(r), s. 42(7)

tested_concepts: s. 42(7) IRCS prerequisites
enumerated offences for IRCS eligibility
mental illness or disorder requirement for IRCS
treatment plan requirement for IRCS

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Review of Youth Sentences — YCJA s. 94
fact_pattern: A 16-year-old is serving a two-year non-custodial youth sentence. After serving six months, he applies for a review of his sentence, asking that the conditions be varied because his circumstances have significantly improved.
call_of_question: Under s. 94(1) of the YCJA, after what period may a young person apply for a review of a non-custodial youth sentence?

options.A: After one year of the sentence has elapsed.
options.B: After six months of the sentence has elapsed or after the person has served one-half of the sentence, whichever comes first.
options.C: At any time, provided there is a material change in circumstances.
options.D: After six months of the sentence has elapsed.

explanation: Under s.94(1) of the YCJA provides that the youth justice court may review a youth sentence imposed on a young person, on application by the young person, the young person's parent, the Attorney General, or the provincial director, if six months have elapsed since the youth sentence was imposed. Section 94(2) allows a review at any time with leave of the youth justice court if there are exceptional circumstances. The standard waiting period before a non-custodial sentence can be reviewed on the young person's application is six months. This reflects the YCJA's emphasis on adaptability — youth sentences can be modified to reflect changed circumstances, but with the six-month threshold to prevent repeated applications disrupting the sentence structure.
why_A_wrong: The statutory period for applying for review under s. 94(1) is six months, not one year. Waiting a full year before applying would exceed the statutory minimum and would not reflect the YCJA's intent to allow timely review of youth sentences.
why_B_wrong: The YCJA does not use a 'six months or one-half of sentence, whichever is earlier' formula for non-custodial sentence reviews under s. 94(1). The threshold is a flat six months from the imposition of the sentence.
why_C_wrong: An application at any time is available only with leave of the court under s. 94(2), and only where there are exceptional circumstances. Without exceptional circumstances, the young person must wait six months before applying for a review. 'Material change in circumstances' describes s. 94(2), not the general s. 94(1) right.
why_D_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 94(1) YCJA, a young person may apply for a review of a non-custodial youth sentence after six months have elapsed since the sentence was imposed.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Review of Youth Sentences — YCJA s. 94
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 94(1), s. 94(2)

tested_concepts: s. 94(1) YCJA — six-month threshold for sentence review
s. 94(2) exceptional circumstances for immediate review
who may apply for review of youth sentence
YCJA sentence flexibility

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: A 13-year-old is arrested by police and taken to the police station for processing. The young person's parents live in the same city and are reachable by phone. Police give the young person an oral caution and release him without charges, relying on an extra-judicial measure.
call_of_question: Under s. 26 of the YCJA, in what circumstances is notice to a parent required, and is it required when police use an extra-judicial measure such as a caution?

options.A: Notice to parents is required only when the young person is charged by information; a police caution does not trigger the notice obligation.
options.B: Notice to parents is required whenever police take any action under the YCJA, including administering a police caution, because s. 26(1) mandates notice upon any arrest or detention.
options.C: Notice to parents is required whenever a young person is detained or is subject to a judicial interim release application; it is not required for an out-of-court police caution.
options.D: Notice to parents is required only if the young person requests it or if the young person is under 12 years of age.

explanation: Under s.26(1) of the YCJA requires that where a young person is arrested and detained in custody, or where a young person is released on an undertaking, a parent must be notified as soon as possible, and the notice must include information about the charge, the arrest, and the right to retain counsel. The notice obligation under s. 26 is triggered by arrest and detention, or by proceedings in court (including release on undertaking). A police caution administered without formal detention or laying of charges does not trigger the s. 26 notice obligation — police cautions and other informal extra-judicial measures are handled outside the formal YCJA notice framework. However, police are encouraged to involve parents in EJM processes as a matter of practice even when not legally required.
why_A_wrong: Notice is not limited to cases where a charge by information is laid. Section 26 also requires notice when a young person is detained or when judicial proceedings are commenced. The trigger is detention or court proceedings, not only the laying of an information.
why_B_wrong: Section 26(1) does not mandate notice upon every police action or contact with a young person. The section is triggered by arrest and detention or by court proceedings — not by every exercise of a police power. An informal police caution used as an EJM does not trigger the s. 26 notice requirement.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 26 requires notice to parents upon detention or court proceedings (including release on undertaking), but a police caution used as an extra-judicial measure without formal detention or court proceedings does not trigger the statutory notice obligation.
why_D_wrong: The notice obligation is not limited to cases where the young person requests it, nor does it have an age-12 threshold. Section 26 applies to any young person (aged 12 to under 18) who is arrested and detained or subject to court proceedings. The right to parental notice is not conditioned on the young person's request.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Notice to Parents — YCJA s. 26
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Youth Criminal Justice Act, S.C. 2002, c. 1, s. 26(1)

tested_concepts: s. 26(1) YCJA — notice obligation triggered by detention or court proceedings
police caution as EJM does not trigger s. 26 notice
notice required upon arrest, detention, or undertaking
distinction between EJM process and formal YCJA proceedings
